agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "The proposal changes delivery ETA granularity from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to how the logistics operation communicates its core promise — when a package arrives — to all downstream stakeholders.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "ETA", "definition": "Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected delivery moment communicated to the recipient and used for operational planning" },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The time range within which a delivery is promised. Currently an exact timestamp; proposed change makes this a full calendar day." },
      { "term": "SLA", "definition": "Service Level Agreement — contractual commitment on delivery timeliness, often measured against the ETA" },
      { "term": "Last Mile", "definition": "The final leg of delivery from depot/hub to the recipient's address" },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery", "definition": "Confirmation that a package was delivered, timestamped against the promised ETA" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Every active delivery must have a communicable ETA visible to the recipient",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery without an ETA is operationally invisible to the customer and breaks the core tracking contract",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery in 'in_transit' or 'out_for_delivery' status, an ETA value is always present and displayable"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "ETA precision must be sufficient to evaluate SLA compliance",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the ETA lacks the precision needed to determine whether an SLA was met or breached, the system cannot enforce contractual obligations or calculate penalties",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an SLA defined with hourly precision (e.g., 'same-day by 18:00'), the ETA representation must retain enough precision to evaluate compliance"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "ETA changes must be auditable with both old and new values recorded",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Audit trail integrity — losing historical ETA precision during migration corrupts the delivery history record",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery whose ETA was updated, the audit log contains both the previous and new ETA values with their original precision"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Internal operational systems may retain higher-precision ETAs than what is displayed to customers",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a display policy decision — ops teams may still need hourly precision for route optimization and driver scheduling even if customers see date-only",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery with a date-only customer ETA, the internal dispatch system still stores and uses a time-precise ETA for routing"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Premium/time-sensitive service tiers (same-day, express, scheduled) must retain time-precise ETAs",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Business policy — premium services are differentiated by time precision; removing it degrades the tier's value proposition. A VP could choose to change this, but it would be a major product decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery with service tier 'express' or 'same-day', the customer-facing ETA includes a time component, not just a date"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A standard-tier delivery is in transit",
      "when": "The recipient views the delivery ETA",
      "then": "The ETA is displayed as a date-only value (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no timestamp"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A premium/express/same-day delivery is in transit",
      "when": "The recipient views the delivery ETA",
      "then": "The ETA retains time-precise display (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') unless explicitly scoped into this change"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "An SLA is defined with hourly precision",
      "when": "The system evaluates SLA compliance for a delivery",
      "then": "The evaluation uses the internal time-precise ETA, not the customer-facing date-only ETA"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A delivery's ETA is migrated from timestamp to date-only for display",
      "when": "The migration occurs",
      "then": "The original timestamp-precision ETA is preserved in the internal/audit data model and not discarded"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "Any API consumer (partner, integration) currently receives timestamp-precision ETAs",
      "when": "The change is deployed",
      "then": "The API contract is versioned — existing consumers continue receiving timestamp-precision ETAs unless they opt into the new format"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A delivery is due 'by Friday'",
      "when": "The system determines if the delivery was on time",
      "then": "The on-time threshold is clearly defined (end of business day? 23:59:59?) and consistently applied"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Delivery spans a timezone boundary (sender in EST, recipient in PST)", "expected_behavior": "'Delivery by Friday' must be evaluated in the recipient's local timezone to avoid a delivery at 11pm EST being shown as 'Saturday' in PST or vice versa" },
    { "scenario": "Same-day delivery displayed as date-only", "expected_behavior": "Saying 'Delivery by today' with no time window is meaningless for same-day — the time IS the product. Must retain time precision or the service tier is indistinguishable from standard." },
    { "scenario": "Delivery ETA is today but it's already 11pm", "expected_behavior": "Date-only display of 'Delivery by today' at 11pm gives false hope. System must handle late-day edge with appropriate messaging (e.g., rescheduled to tomorrow)." },
    { "scenario": "B2B customer with receiving dock hours (e.g., 8am-5pm)", "expected_behavior": "'Delivery by Friday' is insufficient — a truck arriving at 6pm hits a closed dock. B2B customers with dock constraints need time-window ETAs." },
    { "scenario": "Historical reporting on delivery performance", "expected_behavior": "Reports that previously measured on-time performance to the hour must not retroactively lose precision. Historical data must remain at original granularity." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple deliveries to same address on same day", "expected_behavior": "Date-only ETAs make it impossible for recipients to distinguish between two packages both showing 'Delivery by Friday'" },
    { "scenario": "ETA transitions from 'today' to 'tomorrow' at midnight", "expected_behavior": "A package in transit at 11:55pm with a date-only ETA of 'today' that delivers at 12:05am must correctly reflect the date rollover and potential SLA breach" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Mixed. Reducing ETA precision lowers the cost of missed-time penalties (harder for customers to claim 'late' when the window is a full day). However, it degrades the value proposition of premium tiers — if express delivery loses its time guarantee in display, customers have less reason to pay the premium. Risk of revenue loss on premium services if not carefully scoped. Additionally, B2B contracts with time-of-day SLAs may trigger renegotiation or penalties if the system can no longer demonstrate hourly compliance.",
    "operational": "Positive for standard deliveries — dispatchers and drivers face less pressure to hit exact times, reducing route-optimization complexity and lowering the cost of minor delays. Negative if internal systems also lose precision — route planning, driver scheduling, and depot coordination all depend on time-precise ETAs. The change must be display-only, not a data model change, or operational efficiency degrades significantly.",
    "user": "Negative for power users and B2B recipients who plan around delivery times (e.g., someone who took a 2-hour window off work based on the ETA). Mildly positive for users who found exact timestamps misleading when they were inaccurate — a date-only ETA sets a more honest expectation. Net sentiment depends on current ETA accuracy: if timestamps are frequently wrong, date-only may actually increase trust.",
    "risk": "HIGH risk if this change is applied globally without service-tier segmentation. Medium risk of breaking API contracts with partners who consume timestamp ETAs. Low-medium risk of SLA measurement ambiguity — 'delivered by Friday' needs a precise cutoff time defined internally or disputes become unresolvable. Compliance risk if any regulatory requirement mandates time-precise delivery communication (e.g., pharmaceutical, perishable goods).",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End consumers (standard tier)", "impact": "Moderate — lose time precision but gain more realistic expectations. Recipients who plan around delivery times are negatively affected." },
      { "group": "End consumers (premium/express tier)", "impact": "High negative if applied — the time guarantee is the core value they pay for" },
      { "group": "B2B customers with dock/receiving constraints", "impact": "High negative — date-only ETAs are operationally unusable for scheduled receiving" },
      { "group": "Drivers/couriers", "impact": "Mildly positive — reduced pressure to hit exact minute, but dispatch systems still need time precision" },
      { "group": "Dispatch/operations teams", "impact": "Neutral to negative — if internal tools also lose precision, scheduling becomes harder. Must be display-only change." },
      { "group": "API integration partners", "impact": "High if breaking change — partners consuming ETAs for their own SLA calculations will break. Requires versioned rollout." },
      { "group": "Customer support", "impact": "Mixed — fewer 'you said 14:30 and it came at 15:00' complaints, but more ambiguity in resolving 'was it late?' disputes" },
      { "group": "Finance/billing", "impact": "Medium — SLA penalty calculations must be redefined. Late-delivery refund policies need updated criteria." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The proposal is underspecified in several critical dimensions: (1) no indication of which service tiers are in scope, (2) no mention of whether this is a display-only change or a data model change, (3) no definition of what 'by Friday' means operationally (end of business? midnight?), (4) no mention of API contract impact, (5) no mention of how existing SLAs with hourly precision will be evaluated. These gaps make it impossible to confidently approve acceptance criteria without making significant assumptions.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "The change affects SLA evaluation and must not silently reduce internal precision",
      "Premium/express service tiers must be explicitly scoped in or out",
      "API consumers must not receive a breaking change without versioning",
      "Historical data must retain original precision"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Which service tiers are in scope for this change",
      "Whether this is display-only or a data model change",
      "How 'by Friday' is defined as a precise cutoff for SLA purposes",
      "Whether B2B customers with receiving hours are affected",
      "Regulatory or compliance requirements for time-precise ETAs in certain verticals"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "This is intended as a display-layer change only, not a data model change",
      "Premium and express tiers should retain time-precise ETAs",
      "Internal operational systems will continue using timestamp-precision ETAs",
      "Existing API contracts will be versioned, not broken"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The proposal does not specify which service tiers are in scope. Applying date-only ETAs to premium/express/same-day tiers would fundamentally degrade those products. Is this change scoped to standard-tier deliveries only?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Premium customers", "Product team", "Revenue/pricing team"],
      "options": ["A) Apply only to standard-tier deliveries", "B) Apply to all tiers", "C) Apply to standard + economy, exclude express/same-day/scheduled"],
      "recommendation": "Option A or C — premium time-precision is the core differentiator customers pay for"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Is this a display-only change (customer-facing UI/notifications show date-only) or a data model change (the system stores date-only ETAs)? If the latter, route optimization, driver scheduling, and SLA evaluation all break.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations team", "Engineering", "Dispatch"],
      "options": ["A) Display-only — internal systems retain timestamp precision", "B) Data model change — ETAs stored as dates"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — internal precision is critical for operations. This must be a presentation-layer change only."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Delivery by Friday' requires a precise cutoff time for SLA evaluation and on-time metrics. Without this definition, disputes are unresolvable and reporting becomes meaningless. What time constitutes the end of 'Friday'?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customer support", "Finance/billing", "B2B contract managers"],
      "options": ["A) End of business day (e.g., 18:00 local)", "B) End of calendar day (23:59:59 local)", "C) Configurable per customer/contract"],
      "recommendation": "Option B for consumer, Option C for B2B — but this must be explicitly defined before implementation"
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No information provided about existing API consumers who receive timestamp-precision ETAs. A breaking change to API response format could disrupt partner integrations. Need: inventory of API consumers and their ETA field usage.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["API integration partners", "Engineering", "Partner management"],
      "options": ["A) Version the API and maintain backward compatibility", "B) Coordinate migration with all consumers"],
      "recommendation": "Option A — never break existing API contracts without versioning"
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No data provided on current ETA accuracy. If current timestamp ETAs are accurate within minutes, removing precision destroys real value. If they are frequently off by hours, date-only may be more honest. The business case depends entirely on this data.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Provide current ETA accuracy metrics (% on-time within 30min, 1hr, 2hr) to evaluate whether precision reduction is justified"],
      "recommendation": "This data must inform the decision — if ETAs are accurate, reducing precision is value destruction; if inaccurate, it's expectation management"
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
